nodes.name: 20th Century Events
nodes.description: Major world events of the 20th century

items.prompt: Which systematic extermination of the Armenian population in the Ottoman Empire (1915–1916) killed an estimated 600,000–1.5 million people?
items.explanation:
1915–1916; Ottoman government under the Committee of Union and Progress ('Young Turks') targeted Armenian Christian minority
Estimated 600,000–1.5 million Armenians killed through mass shootings, death marches, and starvation in the Syrian desert
Deportation death marches to the Syrian desert were a central method; survivors forced to walk hundreds of miles without food or water
Henry Morgenthau Sr., U.S. Ambassador to the Ottoman Empire, documented the atrocities in dispatches — described 'race extermination'
Turkey has never officially recognized the genocide and disputes the death toll — a major diplomatic flashpoint
The Holocaust: Nazi Germany studied the Armenian Genocide; Hitler allegedly said in 1939 'Who today remembers the extermination of the Armenians?'
United States formally recognized it as a genocide in April 2021 under President Biden
items.cardFront: 1915–1916, Ottoman Empire, Armenians, 600,000–1.5 million killed

items.prompt: Which conflict (1917–1922) between the Bolshevik Red Army and the anti-communist White Army consolidated Soviet power and led to the formation of the USSR?
items.explanation:
1917–1922; Red Army (Bolsheviks) vs. White Army (monarchists, liberals, foreign interveners including USA, UK, France, Japan)
14 foreign nations sent troops to support the Whites; all withdrew by 1922 without decisive effect
Leon Trotsky built the Red Army from scratch — considered the military genius of the Bolshevik victory
Famine of 1921–1922 killed an estimated 5 million; total war-related deaths estimated at 7–12 million including disease and famine
Tsar Nicholas II and his family were executed by Bolsheviks at Yekaterinburg, July 17, 1918, to prevent their rescue
Bolsheviks won control of the former Russian Empire's core; Ukraine, Georgia, Armenia, and others were absorbed into the Soviet state
USSR (Union of Soviet Socialist Republics) formally established December 30, 1922
items.cardFront: 1917–1922, Red Army vs. White Army, USSR founded 1922

items.prompt: Which economic catastrophe, triggered by the U.S. stock market crash of October 1929, spread globally to cause 25% unemployment in the USA, democratic backsliding across Europe, and is considered a contributing cause of World War II?
items.explanation:
1929–1939; the worst economic downturn of the 20th century
Black Tuesday (October 29, 1929): U.S. stock market crashed, erasing billions in paper wealth
U.S. unemployment reached 25% by 1933; GDP fell ~30%; 9,000 banks failed; millions of families lost homes and farms
Smoot-Hawley Tariff (1930): U.S. raised import tariffs; other nations retaliated; global trade collapsed by ~65%
FDR's New Deal (1933–1939): relief, recovery, and reform programs; created Social Security, the SEC, and the FDIC
Hitler exploited German economic desperation (unemployment ~30%) to rise to power — the Depression helped produce WWII
items.cardFront: 1929–1939, stock market crash, 25% unemployment, FDR's New Deal

items.prompt: What name is given to Nazi Germany's systematic genocide (1941–1945) that murdered approximately 6 million Jews and millions of others?
items.explanation:
Systematic, state-sponsored genocide by Nazi Germany under Adolf Hitler; industrial killing peaked 1941–1945
Approximately 6 million Jews murdered — about two-thirds of European Jewry — plus ~6 million others: Roma, disabled people, Soviet POWs, political prisoners, and others
Wannsee Conference (January 20, 1942): 15 senior Nazi officials coordinated the 'Final Solution to the Jewish Question' — systematic murder via death camps
Auschwitz-Birkenau (occupied Poland): the largest death camp; approximately 1.1 million people murdered there, mostly Jews
Einsatzgruppen: mobile killing units murdered ~1.5 million Jews in mass shootings in Eastern Europe before the death camps were established
Nuremberg Trials (1945–1946): 24 Nazi leaders tried for crimes against humanity — established the international legal framework still in use today
items.cardFront: Nazi genocide, 1941–1945, 6 million Jews

items.prompt: Which international military tribunal held in Nuremberg from 1945 to 1946 prosecuted 24 major Nazi war criminals for 'crimes against humanity,' establishing precedents that shaped international law for decades?
items.explanation:
International Military Tribunal conducted by US, UK, France, and USSR in Nuremberg, Germany (November 1945 – October 1946)
24 major Nazi defendants tried; 12 sentenced to death including Hermann Goring (who committed suicide hours before execution)
Established the legal concepts of 'crimes against humanity' and 'crimes against peace' — now foundational in international law
Hermann Goring, Joachim von Ribbentrop, Wilhelm Keitel, and others hanged October 16, 1946
Albert Speer (armaments minister) sentenced to 20 years; Rudolf Hess to life imprisonment
Nuremberg Principles became part of international law; established the precedent for the International Criminal Court (ICC, 2002)
items.cardFront: 1945–1946, Nuremberg, Nazi war crimes, 'crimes against humanity'

items.prompt: Which international organization, founded on October 24, 1945 after World War II to prevent future wars, replaced the failed League of Nations and now has 193 member states?
items.explanation:
Founded October 24, 1945 (observed as UN Day); charter signed by 51 original member states in San Francisco on June 26, 1945
Replaced the League of Nations (1920–1946), which had failed to prevent World War II
Five permanent Security Council members with veto power (P5): USA, UK, France, Russia (originally USSR), and China
Universal Declaration of Human Rights adopted December 10, 1948 — primarily drafted by Eleanor Roosevelt
Currently has 193 member states — essentially every recognized sovereign nation on Earth
Key specialized agencies: WHO (health), UNESCO (education/culture), UNICEF (children's welfare), IMF and World Bank (economics)
items.cardFront: October 1945, 193 members, Security Council

items.prompt: Which 1948–1952 US economic program provided ~$13 billion to rebuild Western European economies devastated by World War II, while also containing Soviet expansion?
items.explanation:
Formally the European Recovery Program; proposed by Secretary of State George C. Marshall in a Harvard commencement speech on June 5, 1947
Provided ~$13 billion (equivalent to ~$150 billion today) to 17 Western European nations, 1948–1952
US motivation: both humanitarian and strategic — prevent economic collapse from driving Western Europe toward communism
Soviet Union refused to participate and pressured Eastern Bloc countries not to participate either
West Germany received $1.4 billion; within a decade became Europe's strongest economy (the Wirtschaftswunder, 'economic miracle')
George Marshall won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1953 for the plan — the only US Army general ever to win the Peace Prize
items.cardFront: 1948–1952, US aid to rebuild post-war Europe

items.prompt: Which August 1947 event — ending nearly 200 years of British colonial rule — created the independent nations of India and Pakistan and was accompanied by the Partition's massive communal violence?
items.explanation:
India became independent August 15, 1947; Pakistan on August 14, 1947 — ending British rule dating to the East India Company's dominance from 1757
Partition divided British India into Hindu-majority India and Muslim-majority Pakistan (including East Pakistan, now Bangladesh)
Partition's violence: 10–20 million people displaced in one of history's largest mass migrations; 200,000–2 million killed in communal violence
Mahatma Gandhi's nonviolent civil disobedience movement (satyagraha) was the primary force for independence
Gandhi was assassinated by Hindu nationalist Nathuram Godse on January 30, 1948, just six months after independence
Jawaharlal Nehru became India's first Prime Minister; Muhammad Ali Jinnah became Pakistan's first Governor-General
items.cardFront: August 1947, Gandhi, India-Pakistan partition

items.prompt: Which state was established on May 14, 1948 when David Ben-Gurion read its Declaration of Independence, prompting the USA to extend recognition within 11 minutes and five Arab armies to invade the next day?
items.explanation:
David Ben-Gurion proclaimed the State of Israel in Tel Aviv on May 14, 1948 as the British Mandate for Palestine expired
President Truman extended U.S. de facto recognition within 11 minutes of the declaration
Five Arab armies (Egypt, Jordan, Syria, Iraq, Lebanon) invaded the next day; 1948 Arab-Israeli War ('Israeli War of Independence')
Israel survived and in armistice agreements (1949) held more territory than the 1947 UN partition plan had allocated
Palestinian refugee crisis: ~700,000 Palestinian Arabs fled or were expelled — called the Nakba ('catastrophe')
Zionist movement founded by Theodor Herzl in 1897; the Holocaust gave the cause international momentum
items.cardFront: David Ben-Gurion, May 14, 1948, declared in Palestine

links.text: David Ben-Gurion, May 14, 1948, declared in Palestine

items.prompt: Which military alliance, founded in April 1949 by 12 Western nations, was designed to provide collective defense against Soviet expansion and remains the world's largest military alliance?
items.explanation:
Founded April 4, 1949; 12 original members: USA, UK, France, Belgium, Netherlands, Luxembourg, Canada, Denmark, Iceland, Italy, Norway, Portugal
Article 5: 'an attack on one is an attack on all' — collective defense principle; invoked only once, after the September 11, 2001 attacks
Soviet Union countered with the Warsaw Pact (1955), a competing alliance of Eastern Bloc nations
Cold War's end led to NATO expansion eastward; former Warsaw Pact nations Poland, Hungary, and Czech Republic joined in 1999
As of early 2024, has 32 members following Sweden's accession in March 2024
NATO's relevance was debated after the Cold War but surged after Russia's invasion of Ukraine (2022)
items.cardFront: April 4, 1949, 12 founding members, collective defense

items.prompt: Which geopolitical rivalry (1947–1991) between the United States and Soviet Union — marked by proxy wars, nuclear arms race, and ideological competition — ended with the Soviet Union's collapse?
items.explanation:
Political and military standoff between the US (NATO) and USSR (Warsaw Pact) from approximately 1947 to 1991
Named 'Cold' because the two superpowers never directly fought each other — open war would have risked nuclear annihilation (MAD: Mutually Assured Destruction)
Berlin was the iconic Cold War battleground: Berlin Airlift (1948–1949), Berlin Wall built (1961), Wall fell (1989)
Nuclear arms race: both sides built thousands of warheads; the US had ~31,000 and the USSR ~40,000 at their peaks
Proxy wars: Korea, Vietnam, Angola, Nicaragua, Afghanistan — superpower rivalry fought through surrogates worldwide
Ended December 25, 1991 when Gorbachev resigned and the Soviet flag was lowered over the Kremlin
items.cardFront: 1947–1991, US vs. USSR, nuclear arms race

items.prompt: Which 1956 crisis erupted when Egypt's Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal, prompting a secret Anglo-French-Israeli invasion that the US and USSR jointly forced to reverse — signaling the end of British and French global dominance?
items.explanation:
Egypt's Gamal Abdel Nasser nationalized the Suez Canal (July 26, 1956), ending British-French control and financing the Aswan Dam
Britain, France, and Israel secretly planned Operation Musketeer: Israel invaded Sinai; Britain and France used it as pretext to occupy the canal zone
US President Eisenhower — furious at being kept in the dark — threatened economic sanctions against Britain and sterling crisis
USSR threatened missile attacks on Paris and London; combined US-USSR pressure forced Britain, France, and Israel to withdraw in humiliation
Mark of the definitive end of British and French status as independent global superpowers; Egypt retained the canal
Nasser emerged as a pan-Arab hero; Eisenhower Doctrine asserted US as the dominant Western power in the Middle East
items.cardFront: 1956, Nasser nationalizes Suez Canal, Britain+France+Israel humiliated

items.prompt: Which process — primarily occurring between 1957 and 1975 — saw most of Africa's nations gain independence from European colonial powers, transforming the continent's political map?
items.explanation:
Most African colonies gained independence 1957–1975; the 'Year of Africa' (1960) saw 17 nations become independent in a single year
Ghana (March 6, 1957), led by Kwame Nkrumah, was the first sub-Saharan African colony to gain independence from Britain — a landmark moment
14 French colonies gained independence simultaneously in 1960 as de Gaulle negotiated managed decolonization
Algerian War of Independence (1954–1962): brutal eight-year conflict; an estimated 300,000–1,000,000 Algerians died fighting French colonial rule
Many newly independent states inherited arbitrary colonial borders that cut across ethnic and linguistic lines — a root cause of ongoing conflicts
South Africa's end of apartheid (1994) is considered the final act of Africa's decolonization
items.cardFront: 1957–1975, Africa, colonial independence movements

items.prompt: Which thirteen-day October 1962 confrontation — after US spy planes discovered Soviet nuclear missiles in Cuba — brought the world to the brink of nuclear war?
items.explanation:
October 16–28, 1962: the closest the Cold War came to becoming a nuclear war
US U-2 spy plane photographed Soviet ballistic missile sites under construction in Cuba on October 14, 1962
President Kennedy imposed a naval 'quarantine' (blockade) of Cuba; Soviet ships carrying missiles turned back after tense standoff
Khrushchev agreed to remove Soviet missiles in exchange for: US pledge not to invade Cuba, and secret removal of US Jupiter missiles from Turkey
Vasili Arkhipov, a Soviet submarine officer on B-59, refused to authorize a nuclear torpedo launch on October 27 — possibly preventing nuclear war
Directly produced the Moscow-Washington hotline ('red phone') and the Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty (1963)
items.cardFront: October 1962, Soviet missiles in Cuba, nuclear brinkmanship

links.text: October 1962, Soviet missiles in Cuba, nuclear brinkmanship

items.prompt: Which political campaign (1966–1976) launched by Mao Zedong mobilized Red Guards to purge 'counter-revolutionary' elements in China, closing universities, destroying cultural heritage, and killing hundreds of thousands?
items.explanation:
1966–1976; officially the 'Great Proletarian Cultural Revolution'; launched by Mao Zedong to reassert his authority within the Communist Party
Mao mobilized millions of young 'Red Guards' to attack the 'Four Olds': old customs, culture, habits, and ideas
Intellectuals, teachers, artists, and party officials were publicly humiliated, imprisoned, tortured, or killed; universities closed for years
Deng Xiaoping (future paramount leader) was purged twice; Lin Biao (Mao's chosen successor) died fleeing to the USSR after a failed coup attempt in 1971
Historical sites, temples, books, and artworks destroyed across China
Ended with Mao's death (September 9, 1976) and the arrest of the 'Gang of Four'; estimated 500,000–2 million deaths
items.cardFront: 1966–1976, Mao Zedong, Red Guards, destroyed Chinese cultural heritage

items.prompt: What historic event occurred on July 20, 1969 when Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin became the first humans to walk on the Moon, with Armstrong declaring it 'one small step for man'?
items.explanation:
July 20, 1969; Apollo 11 mission; lunar module Eagle landed in the Sea of Tranquility
Neil Armstrong: first human to walk on the Moon; said 'That's one small step for man, one giant leap for mankind' (he later stated he intended 'for a man')
Buzz Aldrin joined Armstrong on the surface; Michael Collins orbited in the command module Columbia
Fulfilled JFK's 1961 challenge to land on the Moon 'before this decade is out,' announced less than three weeks after Yuri Gagarin's first human spaceflight
Soviet Luna program had been racing to get there first; the USSR never landed humans on the Moon
Six Apollo missions successfully landed on the Moon (11, 12, 14, 15, 16, 17); last crewed mission was Apollo 17 (December 1972)
items.cardFront: July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong, Apollo 11, Sea of Tranquility

links.text: July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong, Apollo 11, Sea of Tranquility

items.prompt: Which February 1972 visit by President Nixon to China — the first by a sitting US president — reopened relations after 23 years of hostility and shifted Cold War dynamics by splitting the Sino-Soviet bloc?
items.explanation:
President Nixon visited China February 21–28, 1972 — first visit by a sitting US president since the 1949 Communist Revolution
Facilitated by secret preparatory visit by National Security Advisor Henry Kissinger to Beijing in July 1971
Nixon met Chairman Mao Zedong and Premier Zhou Enlai; Shanghai Communique acknowledged Taiwan as part of China ('one China' policy)
'Only Nixon could go to China' — his strong anti-communist record gave him political cover for the diplomatic opening
Split the Sino-Soviet alliance; China and USSR had already clashed militarily at the Ussuri River border (1969)
US and China formally normalized diplomatic relations under President Carter in 1979
items.cardFront: February 1972, Nixon meets Mao, 'only Nixon could go to China'

links.text: February 1972, Nixon meets Mao, 'only Nixon could go to China'

items.prompt: Which political scandal (1972–1974) involving a break-in at the Democratic National Committee headquarters and subsequent cover-up led to the only resignation of a U.S. president?
items.explanation:
June 1972 – August 1974; break-in at the Watergate complex in Washington D.C. by operatives connected to Nixon's re-election campaign
Nixon won re-election in a landslide in November 1972; Watergate initially seemed minor
Senate Watergate Committee hearings (1973) revealed Nixon taped all White House conversations
Saturday Night Massacre (October 20, 1973): Nixon ordered the firing of special prosecutor Archibald Cox; Attorney General and Deputy AG resigned in protest
'Smoking gun' tape (June 23, 1972 recording) proved Nixon ordered the CIA to obstruct FBI investigation — directly implicating him
Nixon resigned August 9, 1974 — the only U.S. president to resign; Gerald Ford succeeded him and controversially pardoned Nixon
Washington Post reporters Bob Woodward and Carl Bernstein broke much of the story, guided by anonymous source 'Deep Throat' (later revealed as FBI Deputy Director Mark Felt)
items.cardFront: 1972–1974, Nixon, Watergate break-in, cover-up, only presidential resignation

items.prompt: Which 1973 action by Arab OPEC members, triggered by US support for Israel in the Yom Kippur War, quadrupled oil prices overnight, caused gasoline rationing, and permanently transformed Western energy policy?
items.explanation:
Arab members of OPEC embargoed oil exports to the US and other countries supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War (October 1973)
Oil prices quadrupled from ~$3 to ~$12 per barrel within months
US gas stations ran dry; long lines for gasoline; odd/even license plate rationing systems imposed in many states
National speed limit reduced to 55 mph to conserve fuel; daylight saving time extended year-round
Revealed deep Western dependence on Middle Eastern oil; led to strategic petroleum reserves, fuel efficiency standards, and energy policy overhaul
Embargo ended March 1974; permanently elevated OPEC as a geopolitical force and accelerated alternative energy research
items.cardFront: 1973, Arab OPEC, Yom Kippur retaliation, oil prices quadrupled

items.prompt: Which April 26, 1986 nuclear reactor explosion in Soviet Ukraine — the worst nuclear disaster in history — contaminated much of Europe and contributed to the Soviet Union's collapse?
items.explanation:
Occurred April 26, 1986 at Reactor No. 4 of the Chernobyl Nuclear Power Plant in Pripyat, Ukrainian SSR
Exploded during a poorly managed safety test: a power surge destroyed the reactor and ignited graphite fires that burned for 10 days
31 people died directly; WHO estimates ~4,000 eventual radiation-related cancer deaths; other studies project higher figures
Pripyat (population 50,000) evacuated 36 hours after the explosion; remains uninhabited today inside the Exclusion Zone
A concrete 'sarcophagus' entombed the reactor in 1986; replaced by the New Safe Confinement arch in 2016
Gorbachev credited Chernobyl with accelerating glasnost — the disaster's scale could not be hidden and destroyed trust in Soviet institutions, helping accelerate the USSR's collapse
items.cardFront: April 26, 1986, Ukraine, worst nuclear disaster

items.prompt: Which June 1989 pro-democracy massacre in Beijing — symbolized by the 'Tank Man' photograph — ended weeks of student-led protests with hundreds to thousands killed by Chinese military forces?
items.explanation:
Protests began April 15, 1989 after the death of reformist leader Hu Yaobang; grew to over 1 million participants in Beijing
Student-led pro-democracy movement occupied Tiananmen Square for weeks; similar protests erupted in over 400 other Chinese cities
Martial law declared May 20, 1989; military crackdown ordered by Deng Xiaoping began the night of June 3–4
Death toll officially ~200–300; estimates from declassified documents and witnesses range from hundreds to over 10,000; Chinese government has never disclosed official figures
'Tank Man': an unknown protester blocking a column of Type 59 tanks on June 5, 1989 — one of the most iconic photographs of the 20th century
Still heavily censored in China; the anniversary ('June Fourth') is scrubbed from Chinese social media annually
items.cardFront: Beijing, June 4, 1989, Tank Man, pro-democracy massacre

links.text: Beijing, June 4, 1989, Tank Man, pro-democracy massacre

items.prompt: Which barrier — built overnight on August 13, 1961 to prevent East Germans from fleeing to the West — fell on November 9, 1989, symbolizing the end of the Cold War?
items.explanation:
Built overnight August 12–13, 1961 by East Germany (GDR) to stop the exodus of 2.5 million East Germans who had fled to the West since 1949
Evolved from barbed wire into a concrete wall 155 km long, with 302 watchtowers, searchlights, and an inner 'death strip' with tripwires and dogs
At least 140 people confirmed killed trying to cross; the actual number may be higher
President Kennedy's 'Ich bin ein Berliner' speech (June 26, 1963) expressed solidarity with West Berliners
President Reagan's 'Mr. Gorbachev, tear down this wall!' speech at the Brandenburg Gate (June 12, 1987)
Fell November 9, 1989 after an East German official mistakenly announced at a press conference that citizens could cross freely immediately; jubilant crowds dismantled it with hammers
items.cardFront: 1961–1989, divided Berlin, Cold War symbol

items.prompt: Which December 25, 1991 event — Gorbachev's resignation and the lowering of the Soviet flag over the Kremlin — formally ended the USSR and created 15 independent successor states?
items.explanation:
Soviet Union (USSR) formally dissolved December 25, 1991, when Gorbachev resigned as president and the Soviet flag was replaced by Russia's tricolor
Gorbachev's reforms — glasnost (openness) and perestroika (restructuring) — had loosened central control and unleashed nationalist movements
Most decisive single event: the failed August 1991 coup attempt by hardliners against Gorbachev; Boris Yeltsin's defiant stand on a tank outside the Russian parliament building rallied public opposition
Baltic states (Estonia, Latvia, Lithuania) declared independence and were recognized internationally in 1991; other republics followed
Created 15 independent states including Russia, Ukraine, Kazakhstan, Belarus, and the Central Asian republics
Russia inherited the USSR's UN Security Council seat, nuclear arsenal, and international legal obligations; Boris Yeltsin became Russia's first president
items.cardFront: December 25, 1991, Gorbachev resigned, 15 successor states

items.prompt: Which system of institutionalized racial segregation — enforced in South Africa from 1948 to 1994 — was dismantled when Nelson Mandela became the country's first democratically elected president?
items.explanation:
Afrikaans for 'separateness'; enforced by the National Party government from 1948, codifying and intensifying earlier racial segregation
Laws classified people as White, Coloured, Indian, or Black (Bantu) and restricted movement, residence, employment, and education by race
Nelson Mandela, leader of the African National Congress (ANC), imprisoned for 27 years (1964–1990) on Robben Island, where he became a global symbol of resistance
International sanctions (sports boycotts, economic embargoes) combined with internal resistance pressured the government
President F.W. de Klerk released Mandela in February 1990 and negotiated the end of apartheid
Mandela won the Nobel Peace Prize (jointly with de Klerk) in 1993; elected President of South Africa on April 27, 1994 — the country's first democratic election
items.cardFront: South Africa, 1948–1994, racial segregation, Mandela

items.prompt: Which 1994 mass killing in Rwanda — in which approximately 800,000 Tutsi and moderate Hutu were murdered in 100 days — was one of the fastest and most concentrated genocides in history?
items.explanation:
April 7–July 15, 1994; Hutu extremists (Interahamwe militia) systematically murdered Tutsi and moderate Hutu civilians
Approximately 800,000 killed in 100 days — roughly 8,000 per day; about 70–75% of Rwanda's Tutsi population was murdered
Triggered by the assassination of President Juvenal Habyarimana on April 6, 1994, when his plane was shot down over Kigali
Radio Milles Collines broadcast Tutsi-dehumanizing propaganda, calling Tutsi 'inyenzi' (cockroaches) and urging killers to 'cut down the tall trees'
UN peacekeeping commander Romeo Dallaire requested permission to intervene; denied; the US, France, and Belgium evacuated only their own citizens
Paul Kagame's Rwandan Patriotic Front ended the genocide by military force; ~2 million Hutu fled to Congo fearing retribution
items.cardFront: Rwanda, 1994, 800,000 killed in 100 days

items.prompt: Which terrorist attacks on September 11, 2001, carried out by 19 al-Qaeda hijackers who crashed planes into the World Trade Center and the Pentagon, killed nearly 3,000 people and launched the U.S. 'War on Terror'?
items.explanation:
September 11, 2001; 19 al-Qaeda operatives hijacked four commercial aircraft
Two planes struck the World Trade Center in New York; both 110-story towers collapsed within 2 hours; ~2,600 killed
One plane hit the Pentagon in Arlington, Virginia; 184 killed
United 93 crashed in Shanksville, Pennsylvania after passengers revolted; 40 killed; intended target believed to be the Capitol or White House
Osama bin Laden of al-Qaeda claimed responsibility; killed by U.S. Navy SEALs in Abbottabad, Pakistan on May 2, 2011
Led directly to the invasion of Afghanistan (October 2001), the Iraq War (2003), the Department of Homeland Security, and the USA PATRIOT Act
items.cardFront: September 11, 2001, al-Qaeda, hijacked 4 planes, ~3,000 deaths
